title-group.article-title: Advancements and challenges in ophthalmic microneedles to treat eye diseases

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: By 2050, more than 61 million people worldwide are expected to lose their vision due to conditions like age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, and uveitis (Bourne et al. 2021). This anticipated rise highlights the urgent need for more effective treatment options. While progress continues in developing new pharmacological agents, treating ocular diseases with these therapies remains particularly challenging due to the eye’s unique and complex anatomy. This is largely due to the limitations of current drug delivery methods, including systemic administration, topical delivery application, transscleral/periocular drug delivery, and intravitreal injections, which are associated with low bioavailability, side effects, and rapid drug clearance. Given these challenges, microneedles have emerged as a promising alternative. Their minimally invasive nature and ability to precisely target the anterior and posterior segments make them well suited for enhancing therapeutic outcomes while reducing systemic exposure and potential side effects, as well as improving patient adherence (Kang-Mieler et al. 2017; Gadziński et al. 2022). The purpose of this review is to discuss recent advancements, key challenges, and strategies for microneedle-based ocular drug delivery systems, with an emphasis on their potential to treat both anterior and posterior eye diseases.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: It is projected that more than 61 million individuals globally will experience vision loss by 2050 due to conditions such as age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, retinitis, and uveitis (Bourne et al. 2021). Vision loss from these disorders adversely impacts physical and mental health, work capacity, daily functioning, and quality of life. Consequently, effective treatments are essential by the development of new pharmacological agents or better delivery of current medications to the eye. Delivering drugs to the eye poses considerable challenges because its unique anatomy and physiology hinder achieving therapeutic drug concentrations at target sites (Kang-Mieler et al. 2017). Current approaches for treating eye diseases include systemic administration, topical drug delivery, transscleral/periocular drug delivery, intravitreal (IVT) injection, and emerging microneedle-based approaches (Figure 1A) (Kang-Mieler et al. 2017; Ham et al. 2023). Systemic administration often requires large drug doses to achieve therapeutic concentrations at target sites, which can result in severe side effects (Huang et al. 2016). Topical delivery, commonly in the form of eye drops, is primarily used to treat anterior segment diseases (Maulvi et al. 2016). Due to barriers such as tear dilution, the lipophilic corneal epithelium, lacrimation, nasolacrimal drainage, and tear turnover, less than 5% of the drug reaches the aqueous humour, leading to low bioavailability (Kang-Mieler et al. 2014). Transscleral or periocular drug delivery targets the posterior segment of the eye and provides better drug permeation compared to the cornea (Ranta and Urtti 2006). Despite this advantage, challenges persist, including rapid drug clearance due to the high blood flow in the choroid and the limitation that only drugs with molecular weights under 70 kDa can effectively penetrate the sclera (Geroski and Edelhauser 2001; Myles et al. 2005). IVT injection is the most direct and efficient method for delivering drugs to the posterior segment (Story et al. 2025). This approach has been used for a range of drugs, including both small and large molecules. However, the IVT injection exposes the retina to an initial high concentration followed by a low concentration, which is not an optimal drug delivery profile (Liu et al. 2019). Additionally, IVT injections can lead to complications such infection, injection-related discomfort, retinal tear (Turturro et al. 2011; Glover et al. 2023). Given these challenges, microneedles have emerged as a promising alternative (Figure 1B). Microneedles can improve drug bioavailability, bypass physiological barriers of eye, and enable controlled drug release (Al-Nimry and Abu Dayah 2024). Microneedles can be used to access the sclera and the suprachoroidal space (SCS), targeting the posterior segment of the eye and offering a less invasive procedure with reduced the complications compared to IVT injections (Jung et al. 2019). In addition, cornea is another route where microneedles can access, targeting the anterior segment with enhanced bioavailability compared to topical drug delivery (Albadr et al. 2022). Microneedles have shown promising results in the treatment of ocular diseases (Table 1). This review highlights recent advances and key challenges in microneedle-based ocular drug delivery systems for treating eye disorders. It explores effective strategies for designing and manufacturing microneedles, offering guidance for researchers in developing these systems for various ocular conditions. Additionally, it emphasises the potential applications of microneedles in addressing both anterior and posterior ocular diseases.

caption.p: (A) Illustration of the ophthalmic drug delivery routes. (B) Different types of ophthalmic microneedles (MNs). (Created by the authors).
Preclinical trials to treat eye diseases.

tr.td: Bevacizumab-loaded silicon MNs were inserted into the cornea of rabbits, effectively inhibiting corneal neovascularization.
Bevacizumab-coated MN was inserted into the corneal stroma of a rabbit model. The MN injection enhanced the suppression of corneal neovascularization compared to eye drops.
Dual drug release of diclofenac and DC 101 from double-layered MNs effectively inhibited the inflammatory response and corneal neovascularization in a mouse model.

Solid dispersion amphotericin B-loaded MNs were inserted into the cornea of an in vivo Candida albicans infection rabbit model. The drug delivery from the MNs demonstrated enhanced antifungal efficacy compared to amphotericin B eye drops and ointment.

Pilocarpine delivery by MNs resulted in significant pupil constriction in a rabbit model, with the diameter reducing from 8.0 mm to 5.0 mm. These promising findings suggest that MNs could offer potential treatment for glaucoma.
Treatment with CLS-TA injected by MNs, along with IVT injection of aflibercept, showed similar benefits to aflibercept monotherapy in treating diabetic macular oedema.

fn.p: MN, microneedle; DC101, monoclonal rat anti-mouse antibody; TA, triamcinolone acetonide; CLS-TA, suprachoroidal triamcinolone acetonide injectable suspension; IVT, intravitreal; NIU, noninfectious uveitis; CST, central subfield thickness; BCVA, best corrected visual acuity.

sec.title: Materials and design principles to fabricate ocular microneedles
Materials
sec.p: Various materials have been explored for the fabrication of ophthalmic microneedles. The materials used for microneedles must have sufficient mechanical strength to penetrate ocular tissue. They should also be biocompatible, non-cytotoxic, and not induce any immune response (Henry et al. 1998). Silicon, metals, and polymers are more commonly used to produce ocular microneedles. The choice of material depends on the specific properties required for their applications. Silicon microneedles, introduced in the 1990s, were the first type of microneedle (Henry et al. 1998). The silicon-based hollow microneedle device, intradermal delivery device (MicronJet™), has been approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as an intradermal drug delivery system (Levin et al. 2015). Advances in micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS) allow precise control over the shape and length of silicon microneedles (Pradeep Narayanan and Raghavan 2017; Le et al. 2023). Silicon microneedles can achieve a high aspect ratio of needle tips and strong mechanical strength with sharp tips through MEMS (Pradeep Narayanan and Raghavan 2017; Le et al. 2023). A recent study showed that silicon nanoneedles, with a base diameter between 0.9 and 3 µm and an aspect ratio ranging from 3 to 66, were inserted into the cornea (Park et al. 2022). The nanoneedles exhibited slow degradation over several months, enabling long-term, sustained drug delivery of bevacizumb to inhibit corneal neovascularization in a rabbit model (Park et al. 2022).

Metals are used to fabricate coated or hollow microneedles, with stainless steel and titanium (Ti) being the most commonly used. Metal microneedles offer several advantages over silicon microneedles. The manufacturing process for metal microneedles is generally more cost-effective, and they also demonstrate superior mechanical strength compared to silicon microneedles (Le et al. 2023). Ti-based microneedles demonstrated promising results in ex vivo cornea insertion tests, suggesting the potential of delivering drug molecules to the eye (Khandan et al. 2016).

Beyond metals, polymers are the most commonly used materials for manufacturing microneedles due to their advantages, including low production costs, ease of fabrication, and design flexibility. This versatility allows for the development of microneedles with a wide range of properties, including dissolving, swellable, and structural characteristics tailored to specific drug delivery needs.

sec.title: Design principles
sec.p: The design of ocular microneedles has been studied to optimise structural features such as the shape, height, and aspect ratio of the needles, the interspacing between each needle, the bevel angle, and the shape of the backing layer. The conical needle shape of polymeric microneedles offered better insertion efficiency compared to other geometries (Y. Li et al. 2020; Fitaihi et al. 2024). The height of microneedles typically ranges from 200 to 1100 µm, depending on the application to the cornea, sclera, or SCS. Published studies suggest that the optimal aspect ratio (length to base diameter) of polymeric microneedles for insertion into cornea or sclera is between 2 and 2.5 (Roy et al. 2019; Aumklad et al. 2022; Wu et al. 2022; Fitaihi et al. 2023; Wu et al. 2023; Kong et al. 2023). The interspacing between each needle should be greater than 150 µm to prevent the bed-of-nails effect, which can hinder tissue insertion (Olatunji et al. 2013). The tip angle also influences insertion efficiency. Hejri et al. tested the insertion ability of microneedles with different bevel angles of 30°, 45°, 55°, and 65° and found that a 55° tip angle allowed for low-resistance injection into the SCS without any leakage (Hejri et al. 2023). Depending on the insertion sites, different shapes of backing layers for polymeric microneedles should be considered. Microneedles with a planar backing layer are suitable for use on the scleral surface (Roy et al. 2022). However, they may not make proper contact with the curved corneal surface (Bhatnagar et al. 2018). To address this, a new microneedle design with a concave-shaped backing, similar to the fit of contact lenses, has been developed for better corneal contact (Roy et al. 2020; Datta et al. 2022).

Polymer-based microneedles should also be designed to provide sufficient durability to meet the therapeutic needs of ocular diseases. They can modulate drug release kinetics, allowing for either rapid or sustained release of drug after insertion into the eye. This control can be achieved by adjusting material composition, encapsulating drugs within delivery vehicles, or incorporating new matrices such as hydrogels. For treatments that require immediate action, short half-life microneedles can deliver the drug quickly upon application, achieving high concentration levels to effectively address conditions like eye infections. Recent studies have utilised hydrophilic polymers such as hyaluronic acid (HA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), and polyethylene glycol to create dissolving microneedles with suitable mechanical strength to penetrate ocular tissues and enable rapid drug release within a few minutes (Albadr et al. 2022; Datta et al. 2022).

In contrast, longer drug half-life is ideal to treat chronic eye diseases such as age-related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, chronic uveitis. By enhancing the half-life of microneedles, it is possible to extend their residence time and sustain drug release at targeted sites. This improvement can help reduce several side effects associated with repeated injections of ocular drugs and high dosages, ultimately enhancing patient compliance. To achieve this, drugs can be incorporated within hydrogels and slowly degradable polymers, or combined with drug delivery vehicles. Hydrogels control drug release kinetics by regulating water absorption and swelling properties, which are influenced by the crosslinking density, composition and concentrations of matrix materials (Yi et al. 2024; Amer and Chen 2020b). A recent study developed hydrogel based microneedles for the recovery of corneal nerve damage (Feng et al. 2025). The needles are composed of hyaluronic acid methacryloyl (HAMA) loaded with recombinant human nerve growth factor (rhNGF). The study found that the release kinetics of fluorescein isothiocyanate-conjugated bovine serum albumin (FITC-BSA), which used as a model for rhNGF, were modulated by the HAMA concentrations. FITC-BSA was released slowly from the microneedles in PBS at 37 °C and the its release rate decreased as the HAMA concentration increased from 15% to 30%. About 60% of FITC-BSA was released from the 15% HAMA hydrogel microneedles within 7 days, while less than 20% of FITC-BSA was released from the 30% HAMA hydrogel microneedles.

In addition, their release kinetics can be controlled by copolymerisation and blending, enabling sustained and long-term drug (range of 0.5–3 months) delivery through hydrolysis and degradation (Pawley et al. 2021; Lee et al. 2021; Łysik et al. 2022; Chen et al. 2023). PCL can be blended with polyethylene glycol (PEG) to modulate drug release. In a study, minoxidil used to promote hair growth and treat high blood pressure was incorporated into various blends of PCL and PEG (Bader and Abu Ammar 2024). The blend with the lowest PEG ratio (PEG 0.5) contains 0.75 mg of PEG and 7.5 mg of PCL, while the blend with the highest PEG ratio (PEG 1) contains 1.5 mg of PEG and 7.5 mg of PCL. The results showed that approximately 50% of the minoxidil was released from the PEG 1 microneedles within 1 day in a PBS solution (pH 7.4, 37 °C), while about 40% was released from the PEG 0.5 microneedles over a period of 20 days.

Encapsulating drugs in various drug carriers allows better control of release rates, as well as enhanced bioavailability and permeability to the target site in ocular applications (Li et al. 2023). In a recent study, ovalbumin (OVA) was used as a model for ranibizumab due to their similar molecular weights in the development of ocular microneedles (Wu et al. 2021). The OVA was encapsulated into PLGA nanoparticles with different primary sonication times ranging from 10 to 60 sec to obtain sustain release behaviour. Results showed that about 27.5% of OVA was released from the PLGA nanoparticles with 10 sec primary sonication for 77 days in PBS (pH 7.4, 0.05% w/v sodium azide, 37 °C), while only about 8% of OVA was released from PLGA nano particles with 60 sec primary sonication. The OVA nanoparticles loaded microneedles demonstrated superior mechanical strength and insertion properties into ex vivo porcine sclera, as well as good retention time.

sec.title: Fabrication of microneedles
sec.p: Recent advancements in microfabrication technology have enabled the production of microneedles. Several techniques are used for microneedle fabrication, including laser cutting, micromechanical machining, photolithography, 3D printing, and drawing lithography (Figure 2).

(B) CNC machining (Pham et al. 2024). Reprinted from Pham et al. (2024) with permission from Springer Nature. (C) Photolithography (Terashima et al. 2020) Reprinted from Terashima et al. (2020) with permission from Springer Nature. (D) Micromolding (Tucak et al. 2020). Reproduced from Tucak et al. (2020) CCBY (2020), MDPI. (E) 3D printing such as stereolithogrpahy (SLA) and digital light processing (DLP) (Sirbubalo et al. 2021). Reproduced from Sirbubalo et al. (2021) CCBY (2021), MDPI. (F) Drawing lithography (Terashima et al. 2020). Reprinted from Terashima et al. (2020) with permission from Springer Nature.

sec.title: Laser cutting
sec.p: The laser cutting technique has been applied to fabricate microneedles from metal and polymer sheets. Metal, such as stainless steel or titanium sheets, is cut based on computer-aided design to obtain the desired size, geometry, and features using a laser (Figure 3A) (Gill and Prausnitz 2007a). Following this, the prepared plates are bent 90 degrees vertically from the plane of the base. In case of metal sheets, the thickness of the microneedles can be reduced through the process of electropolishing, resulting in sharp tips (Gill and Prausnitz 2007a, Gupta et al. 2011; Gill and Prausnitz 2007b).

caption.p: Ocular microneedles fabricated by various methods and their applications. (A) Solid microneedles created using laser cutting techniques (Gill and Prausnitz 2007a). Reprinted from Gill and Prausnitz (2007) with permission from Elsevier. (B) Illustration of the micromolding process to fabricate the microneedle ocular patch (Datta et al. 2022). Adapted from Datta et al. (2022) with permission from Elsevier. (C) (i) Digital images of microneedles with the printed angle of 67.5° by SLA. (ii) Digital photograph of PVA/PVP microneedles alongside a British one-penny coin (diameter = 20.3 mm). (iii) Insertion test of microneedles on porcine sclera and cornea (Fitaihi et al. 2024). Reproduced from Fitaihi et al. (2024) CC-BY (2024), Elsevier. (D) (i) Schematic illustration of the fabrication process for microneedles using reverse drawing lithography. Images of rabbit eyes evaluating phenylephrine-induced pupil dilation following (ii) PBS injection via the microneedle, (iii) phenylephrine injection via the microneedle, and (iv) phenylephrine injection via a 30 G hypodermic needle (Lee et al. 2013). Reprinted with permission from Lee et al. (2013), copyright© 2012 John Wiley and Sons.

sec.title: Micro-mechanical machining
sec.p: Computer numerical control (CNC) machining is a widely used manufacturing process, particularly for creating master moulds from materials such as aluminium, titanium, stainless steel, brass, and polymers for microneedle fabrication (Bediz et al. 2014). It does not require an expensive cleanroom setup. CNC offers precision, tolerance, and reproducibility by using numerical control to manage the fabrication process. The CNC machines move cutting tools along the X, Y, and Z axes of the material blocks based on the geometry code generated by the computer-aided design programme. This movement allows for the precise creation of complex 3D structures. Several studies have optimised CNC machine parameters to fabricate microneedles or their positive moulds with sharp tips and accurate dimensions (Bediz et al. 2014; Malek-Khatabi et al. 2023; Pham et al. 2024). Once the positive mould is made using CNC machines, polymeric microneedles can be produced using the micro-moulding process (Pham et al. 2024). Recently, CNC machining was used to create a stainless steel master mould for fabricating cyclosporine A-loaded polymeric microneedle ocular patches (Figure 3B) (Datta et al. 2022).

sec.title: Photo lithography
sec.p: Photolithography is a technique used to create patterns on a thin film or wafer for microfabrication and has been applied to produce solid and coated microneedles for ocular drug delivery systems (Khandan et al. 2012). Titanium-based microneedles can be fabricated through this process. First, a SiO2 layer was deposited on the surface of a 50-μm-thick commercially pure titanium sheet using plasma-enhanced chemical vapour deposition. Photoresist patterns for the microneedles were then developed on the SiO2 layer’s surface. These patterns were transferred into the SiO2 layer as a masking layer through dry etching. The titanium substrate was etched using the SiO2 mask with the titanium inductively coupled plasma deep etch process. The titanium microneedle was obtained after the SiO2 mask was removed. Additionally, polymeric microneedles can be fabricated using micro-moulding process with the positive mould created by photolithography (Pradeep Narayanan and Raghavan 2017).

sec.title: 3D printing
sec.p: 3D printing creates microneedle structures layer by layer based on a computer-aided design model. The process involves deposition, solidification, and polymerisation of materials under controlled conditions, followed by post-processing. 3D printing can be used to fabricate microneedles through both direct and indirect methods (Pere et al. 2018; Caudill et al. 2021; Liu et al. 2024). The indirect 3D printing method, which uses a two-stage ‘Print & Fill’ mould fabrication procedure, is more commonly used to produce microneedles (Krieger et al. 2019). The first step in fabricating a dissolvable microneedle array is using a 3D printer to create a positive mould. The next step is micro-moulding process using the 3D printed mould. In detail, a silicone polymer replica (polydimethylsiloxane) is then made from this positive mould, and biodegradable microneedles are cast from the silicone mould using various polymers. 3D printing is especially useful for rapid prototyping in early research stages, as it allows for easy adjustments to microneedle designs to achieve the best configurations. Such modifications can be made quickly, cost-effectively, and with high precision (Than et al. 2018). Various 3D printing techniques, including fused deposition modelling (FDM), stereolithography (SLA), and digital light processing (DLP), have been used to fabricate microneedles (Jung and Jin 2021). FDM is an additive 3D printing technique where thermoplastic materials, in filament form, are heated to their softening point and then deposited layer by layer onto a build plate, where they solidify quickly (Jamróz et al. 2018). In one study, FDM was used to create polylactic acid (PLA) microneedles (Luzuriaga et al. 2018). By immersing the 3D-printed cylindrical PLA polymer in potassium hydroxide (KOH) at different intervals, the desired needle shape was achieved. After 9 h of etching in 5 M KOH, the resulting microneedles had widths ranging from 200 μm to 300 μm and tip diameters between 1 μm and 55 μm. SLA, another additive 3D printing method, uses a high-energy light source, such as UV radiation, to polymerise photoactive monomers layer by layer. After the 3D-printed parts are obtained, post-processing involves removing unpolymerized resin residues and UV curing. Recent studies have shown that high-resolution, high-dimensional microneedles can be fabricated using an SLA printer by optimising printing angle and layer thickness. These microneedles have demonstrated the ability to penetrate tissue (Choo et al. 2022; Jeong et al. 2023). One study reported the fabrication of PVP/PVA biodegradable microneedles using a mould printed by SLA for an ocular drug delivery system (Figure 3C) (Fitaihi et al. 2024). These microneedles were able to penetrate scleral and corneal tissues with gentle thumb pressure. DLP uses photosensitive resins such as monomers and oligomers that undergo polymerisation. In DLP, the light projector flashes the cross-section of the entire object as volumetric pixels, curing each layer with a single exposure. Microneedles fabricated by DLP also allow tissue penetration for biomedical applications (Mathew et al. 2021; Bagde et al. 2024).

sec.title: Drawing lithography
sec.p: Drawing lithography takes advantage of the viscosity characteristics of a polymer during the glass transition phase. During this phase, the polymer undergoes elastic deformation, allowing the creation of a 3D microneedle structure through stretching and deformation (Lee and Jung 2012). The process starts with a molten polymer on a baseplate, which then contacts drawing pillars. The polymer is vertically stretched between the baseplate and pillars to form needle-shaped structures. Temperature control is used to properly cure and shape the microneedles. Drawing lithography can also be employed to create hollow metallic microneedles (Lee et al. 2010). In this process, a commercial SU-8 photoresist is applied to the substrate and drawn by micropillars, followed by a curing step to form the microneedle-like structure. The microneedles are then metalized through nickel electroplating. The hollow nickel microneedles were created by removing the resin with the resin remover. Another method, droplet-born air blowing (DAB), was introduced to address the limitations of conventional microneedle fabrication, such as the inactivity of drug molecules by UV light and heat during the process (Lee et al. 2020). In DAB, microneedles are fabricated without the use of UV light or heat. The solution is dispensed onto both the upper and lower plates, and the upper plate is moved toward the lower plate, allowing the droplets to contact each other. The upper plate then moves upward, elongating the viscous solution, and air is blown to remove residual water and solidify the droplets, forming the desired shape. A study reported that a tower microneedle was fabricated using reverse drawing lithography for ocular drug delivery (Figure 3D) (Lee et al. 2013). The microneedle had a tip with an inner diameter of 30 μm, an outer diameter of 90 μm, and a length of 5 mm for minimally invasive injection. Phenylephrine was injected using both traditional hypodermic needles and the tower microneedle through the pars plana of rabbit eyes to compare drug efficacy. The tower microneedles resulted in better pupil dilation in rabbit eyes compared to the traditional hypodermic needles.

sec.title: Different types of microneedles and their applications in the treatment of ocular diseases
sec.p: Microneedles are categorised based on their drug delivery mechanisms, including coated, hollow, dissolving, and swellable types. These microneedles have shown promising outcomes for ocular drug delivery applications (Table 2).

caption.p: Various types of microneedles for ocular drug delivery systems.

tr.td: The drug delivered from the MNs reached the vitreous humor in an ex vivo porcine model. By introducing the coating material, dexamethasone sodium phosphate showed controlled drug release behaviour.

AMP-B-loaded MNs showed in vitro antifungal activity against Candida albicans and Aspergillus niger, while showing no cytotoxic effects on adult retinal pigment epithelial 19 cells. The AMP-B was released from the MNs and detected at a depth of 400 µm in ex vivo porcine corneal tissue.

MNs were inserted into the ex vivo sclera of the porcine eye globe, and dexamethasone-loaded microparticles were released. Approximately 42.4%, 19.2%, 25.9%, and 12.5% of dexamethasone-loaded microparticles were found at the insertion site, in the vitreous humor, in the sclera (excluding the insertion site), and in the anterior segment, respectively.
OVA as a model of ranibizumab
MNs penetrated ex vivo porcine scleral tissue and showed a higher permeation rate of OVA compared to eye drops and needle-free patches.
MNs loaded with besifloxacin were inserted to a depth of 200 µm into excised human corneas. The MNs showed antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus.
Mould prepared by CNC machineMNs: Micromodling

The MOP penetrated and dissolved within an exercised porcine cornea releasing the CsA in a controlled manner. The greatest amount of CsA was retained within the cornea after 30 and 120 min of application.

MNs delivered a larger quantity of pilocarpine compared to the solution formulation on the excised human cornea. The use of MNs improved the permeation of pilocarpine into the aqueous humor of the ex vivo porcine eye globe compared to the solution formulation.

Difluprednate was released from the MNs through hydrolytic degradation and diffusion. The MNs penetrated the porcine sclera and demonstrated a significantly higher permeation rate compared to difluprednate ophthalmic emulsion.
Swellable MNs with interlocking features enhanced the adhesion strength on ex vivo rabbit eyes compared to microneedles without the interlocking features.
Light-responsive and swellable MNs swelled after insertion into an artificial eyeball and shrank upon UV light irradiation, allowing for easy extraction.
Rhodamine B as a model small drug molecule
Rhodamine B, as a model small drug molecule, is uniformly coated on the MN. The MN penetrated to a depth of approximately 400 µm into the ex vivo rabbit cornea.

The pilocarpine-loaded MN was inserted into the in vivo rabbit cornea. The MN system demonstrated better drug bioactivity than the topical drug delivery system. The microneedle caused more pupil constriction within a shorter time than topical drug delivery.
Injected by the hollow MN, particles sized between 20 nm and 10 μm remained in the suprachoroidal space and choroid of a rabbit model for several months without any adverse effects.

fn.p: MN, microneedle; Eudragit® E100, a cationic copolymer based on dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate, butyl methacrylate, and methyl methacrylate; PVP K30, polyvinyl pyrrolidone K30; PVP, poly(vinylpyrrolidone); CsA, cyclosporine A; MOP, microneedle ocular patch; PVA, polyvinyl alcohol; PLGA, poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid); PAA, poly(acrylic acid); HA, hyaluronic acids; AMP-B, amphotericin B; OVA, ovalbumin; SS, stainless steel; CP Ti, commercially pure titanium.

sec.title: Solid microneedles
sec.p: Solid microneedles are usually fabricated with silicon and metal materials (Jiang et al. 2023). Solid microneedles create microchannels in the cornea or sclera, enhancing drug permeability. Typically, a coating is applied to the solid microneedle to load and deliver drugs.

sec.title: Coated microneedles
sec.p: Coated microneedles are designed to deliver drugs through surface coating of solid microneedles. Once the microneedles are inserted into cornea or sclera, the coating dissolves within the ocular tissue, facilitating efficient drug delivery to the targeted areas. The surface coating can be accomplished using various techniques, including electrohydrodynamic atomisation, inkjet coating, gas jet drying, brushing, spray coating, and dip coating (Jiang et al. 2023). Achieving a uniform coating layer ensures consistent drug activity, stable storage, and accurate drug release behaviour. However, they can face challenges such as limited drug-loading capacity and reduced sharpness of needle tips (Parhi 2022). A variety of studies have contributed to the development of coated ocular microneedles. The delivery capabilities of various types of molecules, including drugs, proteins, and DNA, have been evaluated using an in vivo rabbit cornea model with the coated microneedles (Jiang et al. 2007). The results demonstrated that these molecules can be effectively delivered through intrascleral and intracorneal routes, avoiding complications associated with systemic administration and intraocular injections and systemic administration. In a study, a coating layer made of Rhodamine B, a dye, and PVA was uniformly applied to the titanium implant using a dip coating method (Khandan et al. 2016). The results indicate that effective intracorneal delivery can be achieved using coated microneedle, as shown by the deposition of Rhodamine B at the site of tissue insertion. Another study reported that bevacizumab-loaded microneedle effectively suppressed corneal neovascularization (Figure 4A) (Kim et al. 2014). The microneedle was coated with bevacizumab using a dip coating technique. It was successfully inserted into the corneal stroma of the rabbit eye. This method demonstrated superior inhibition of corneal neovascularization when compared to the bevacizumab eye drop treatment, without side effects. The dip coating technique was also used to explore therapeutic outcomes of sunitinib malate in the treatment of corneal neovascularization. Sunitinib malate was coated onto a microneedle pen using the dip coating technique and applied to an in vivo corneal neovascularization mouse model. The results demonstrated that the microneedle pen had greater inhibitory effects on corneal angiogenesis compared to 30 G needle tip (needle tip) (Figure 4B) (Song et al. 2015). These findings suggest that sunitinib malate can be delivered more effectively in a localised minimally invasive manner through cornea using the microneedle pen, compared to hypodermic needles.

caption.p: Ocular applications of coated microneedles. (A) (i) Optical image of a bevacizumab-coated microneedle. Scale bar: 200 μm. (ii) Fluorescence image of the rabbit cornea after removal of an AlexaFluor 750-labelled bevacizumab-coated microneedle. (iii) Histological section of the rabbit cornea. (iv) Histological images showing no difference between sutured eyes with and without bevacizumab-coated microneedle treatment. (v) Bevacizumab-coated microneedles inhibited suture-induced corneal neovascularization in a rabbit model. Scale bar: 500 μm (Kim et al. 2014). Reprinted from Kim et al. sec.title: Hollow microneedles
sec.p: Hollow needles are typically made from materials such as stainless steel, glass, and silicon. They are designed to deliver active agents through their hollow channels directly to the injection site. Hollow microneedles function as conduits for liquid formulations, allowing precise delivery to specific ocular regions. Several studies have contributed to the development of hollow microneedles for ocular drug delivery systems. Intrastromal corneal injections using the hollow microneedle have demonstrated promising results for corneal gene therapy (Figure 5A) (Gilger et al. 2020). The hollow microneedle led to less leakage of adeno-associated viral (AAV) vector genes at the injection site, and more AAV vector genomes were detected in the cornea with reduced exposure to non-target tissues compared to the 31-G needle. Another effective target area for the hollow microneedles is the SCS (Figure 5B) (Kansara et al. 2022). Suprachoroidal (SC) injection can achieve broader bioavailability across the retina and choroid by bypassing barriers such as the internal limiting membrane and vitreous. The SC injection can also limit drug exposure to the anterior segment, which can lower the risk of complications such as elevated intraocular pressure (IOP) and cataract formation, especially with the use of corticosteroids (Wu et al. 2023). Successful delivery of agents has been achieved with hollow microneedles that are 800 to 1000 μm in length, utilising applied pressures between 250 and 300 kPa (Patel et al. 2011). Another study demonstrated that using hollow microneedles to inject sulprostone or brimonidine reduced the required dosage by two orders of magnitude while achieving similar IOP reductions in a rabbit model, compared to topical delivery methods (Kim et al. 2014). In this study, a 720 μm-long microneedle was used, and red fluorescent microparticles were delivered into the SCS to confirm targetability of this hollow needle to the SCS (Figure 5C). This finding suggests that targeted delivery into SCS via hollow microneedles can enhance safety by minimising side effects. Similarly, using a hollow microneedle, triamcinolone (TA) was administered into the SCS of porcine eyes (Figure 5D) (Gilger et al. 2013). TA injections, both low-dose (0.2 mg) and high-dose (2.0 mg), administered by hollow microneedle, successfully reduced acute inflammation in the posterior segment. In addition, Clearside Biomedical has developed a hollow microneedle based drug delivery system, composed of SCS Microinjector® (Clearside Biomedical) and triamcinolone acetonide injectable suspension (XIPERE®, Clearside Biomedical) (Ciulla and Cunningham 2024). It is the first FDA approved microneedle based ocular drug delivery system to treat macular oedema associated with uveitis. These results imply that the translational potential of hollow microneedles in ocular disease management.

(ii) Colour images of the injection site in a rabbit cornea by a 31-G needle and PCI microneedle. The injection caused temporary corneal cloudiness, which was resolved by the first day post-injection. (Gilger et al. 2020). Reproduced from Gilger et al. (2022) with permission from Elsevier. (C) (ⅰ) Image of a 720 μm-long hollow microneedle (white arrow) alongside a liquid eye drop. (ii) Fluorescent image of a human cadaver eye injected with red fluorescein microparticles into the SCS (Kim et al. 2014). Reprinted from Kim et al. sec.title: Dissolving microneedles
sec.p: Dissolving microneedles are fabricated from biodegradable, biocompatible polymers that dissolve or hydrolyse upon application, gradually releasing the drugs. After the microneedles are inserted into the target ocular tissue, they will dissolve upon contact with eye fluid. Drugs will be released from the degraded microneedles (Wu et al. 2023). Dissolving microneedles made from biocompatible polymers do not cause cytotoxicity and do not generate biohazardous sharp waste once they have dissolved (Cheung and Das 2016). The release kinetics of the drugs can be modulated by modifying the composition and material properties of the polymer matrix or by encapsulating the drug in delivery vehicles before loading it into the microneedles (Wu et al. 2021; Lyu et al. 2023). According to recent studies, dissolving microneedles have shown promising results for delivering drugs to the eye. Fluorescein sodium (FS), a model hydrophilic compound, was loaded into dissolving microneedles composed of PVA/methyl acrylate (MA) copolymers (Gantrez® S-97) and HA polymer (Figure 6A) (Suriyaamporn et al. 2021). The microneedles were inserted approximately 200–400 μm into porcine scleral tissue with a force of ~0.08 N per needle and completely dissolved within 2 min. A recent study reported that solid dispersion amphotericin B degradable microneedles (DMN-SD-AMB), composed of PVA and PVP, exhibited antifungal activity (Figure 6B) (Mahfud et al. 2023). The solid dispersion of amphotericin B (SD-AMB) demonstrated a higher release rate than pure AMB, as PVP in SD-AMB acts as a hydrophilic carrier. The microneedles exhibited a fast dissolution rate, dissolving within 75 s. DMN-SD-AMB showed the highest antibacterial efficacy in fungal keratitis rabbit models compared to AMB eye drops and AMB ointment. Than et al. developed double-layered microneedles a rapid release of diclofenac as an anti-inflammatory agent, followed by sustained release of DC101, an anti-angiogenic monoclonal antibody, which was observed for 5 days (Figure 6C) (Than et al. 2018). The microneedle shell is composed of crosslinked methacrylated hyaluronic acid (MeHA), while the core is composed of HA. These microneedles were rapidly implanted into the mouse cornea after insertion and demonstrated approximately a 90% reduction in corneal neovascularization. According to a previous study, two different types of dissolving microneedles, microneedle scleral patches (MSPs) and microneedle corneal patches (MCPs), have been proposed as ocular drug delivery systems depending on target sites (Figure 6D) (Roy et al. 2022). MCPs and MSPs were gently inserted into ex vivo porcine cornea and sclera, respectively, using finger pressure for 5 minutes. Both types of microneedles dissolved within 60 s after insertion into the eye tissue. In addition, MSPs showed a significantly increased TA disposition in posterior segment of rabbit eyes compared to MCPs and TA nanosuspension eye drops. A recent study incorporated amphotericin B (AMP-B) into biodegradable microneedles made from PVP and HA to treat fungal eye infections (Albadr et al. 2022). These microneedles successfully delivered AMP-B through 400 µm of porcine corneal layers, demonstrating excellent biocompatibility with human retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) cells and effective antifungal activity against Candida albicans and Aspergillus niger. In a similar study, AMP-B-loaded liposomes were incorporated into microneedles made from PVP K-30 and PVA (Roy et al. 2019). These microneedles exhibited enhanced antifungal efficacy in an in vivo rabbit model of Candida albicans-induced infection compared to liposomal AMP-B solution. Another study found that fluconazole (FLUZ) microemulsions loaded into microneedles showed promising results for treating fungal keratitis (Suriyaamporn et al. 2022). The microneedles had an outer layer composed of 3% chitosan and 20% PVA, while the inner layer contained FLUZ microemulsion, eugenol, water, poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) 400, and Tween 80. These microneedles penetrated to a depth of 213.7–271.1 μm in porcine corneas. The microneedles demonstrated superior antifungal efficacy against Candida albicans in an ex vivo porcine model compared to FLUZ suspension. Additionally, FLUZ-loaded PLA and HA microneedles exhibited antifungal activity against Fusarium solani (Shi et al. 2022). In a rabbit fungal keratitis model, these microneedles effectively reduced the fungal load compared to the group treated with eye drops. They also significantly decreased macrophage and leucocyte infiltration as well as levels of inflammatory cytokines. Based on these resent studies, dissolving microneedles have demonstrated remarkable results in both in vitro and in vivo experiments for ocular applications. However, clinical validation of dissolving microneedles is still incomplete, and it is necessary for their use in ophthalmology. Another concern is the potential risk of incomplete microneedle insertion due to decreased mechanical strength resulting from water exposure during insertion (Ando et al. 2024).

sec.title: Swellable microneedles
sec.p: A swellable microneedle is made from swelling biocompatible polymer and composed of three-dimensional polymer structures that can encapsulate drug molecules (Sil et al. 2024). These polymers contain hydrophilic groups such as ─NH2, ─OH, ─COOH, ─SO3H, ─CONH2, and ─CONH, and can create channels for drug delivery by absorbing water from ocular fluids upon injection (Donnelly et al. 2014). Such swelling properties of hydrogels can be modulated by changing pore size, porosity and degree of crosslinking which will control the drug release kinetics (Dimatteo et al. 2018; Aroche et al. 2025). However, some concerns exist regarding the use of swellable microneedles for ocular drug delivery. The hydrated nature of the eye could cause the microneedles to swell during injection, potentially leading to insertion failure due to reduced mechanical strength and tip sharpness. In addition, unreacted polymers after chemical crosslinking may also lead to cytotoxicity in the eye (Turner et al. 2021). Despite these concerns, promising results for swellable microneedles have been reported as an alternative to intravitreal injections in ocular drug delivery systems. As an example, PVA based hydrogel microneedle arrays have demonstrated the sustained drug release behaviour for up to 1 month in the drug release model mimicking vitreous humor fluid. The swellable microneedle arrays show promise for treating chronic eye diseases (Amer and Chen 2020a). Furthermore, interlocking features have been incorporated into the swellable PVA microneedles to ensure they remain more securely attached to ex vivo rabbit eye compared to microneedles without such features (Figure 7A) (Amer and Chen 2020b). However, such locking features can increase extraction forces to remove microneedles after drug delivery is completed, potentially resulting in damage to eye. To address this concern, stimuli-sensitive bifunctional swellable microneedles composed of PVA and spiropyran-conjugated N-isopropylacrylamide were introduced to reduce the extraction force (Amer et al. 2022). The swellable microneedles were inserted into a sclera-mimicking phantom, an artificial eye model and shrank upon UV light irradiation, allowing for easy extraction (Figure 7B).

caption.p: Ocular applications of swellable microneedle. Ocular applications of swellable microneedles. (A) (i) Conceptual illustration of the interlocking feature in swellable microneedles. (ii) Swelling behaviour of microneedles immersed in a vitreous humor-mimicking gel (Amer and Chen 2020b).Reprinted from Amer and Chen (2020) with permission from John Wiley and Sons. (B) (i) Illustration of the application of stimuli-sensitive bifunctional swellable microneedles on the eye. (ii) Experimental setup for force measurement of the microneedles on a sclera-mimicking phantom, an artificial eye model. (iii) Extraction force measurement of microneedle (Amer et al. 2022). Reprinted from Amer et al. (2022) with permission from ASME.

sec.title: Clinical trials of ocular microneedles
sec.p: Completed clinical trials have been exploring the potential applications of ophthalmic microneedles (Table 3). Clearside Biomedical is a leading company that has developed a single microneedle injector system, which has progressed to clinical trial stages for ocular drug delivery. In addition, the administration of triamcinolone acetonide injectable suspension (Clearside Biomedical, USA) using the SCS Microinjector, a type of single hollow microneedle, has been approved by FDA. However, other types of microneedles have not yet been evaluated in clinical trials for ocular applications. There are several challenges to the successful translation of ocular microneedles into clinical practice. Appropriate regulatory guidelines need to be established for their use. Currently, FDA has provided only for dermal microneedles, and no similar guidelines exist for ocular use (Mulkutkar et al. 2024). This regulatory gap has hindered the initiation of clinical trials for ocular microneedle technologies. Most microneedles will be considered drug-device combination products, which adds complexity in the approval process. For example, the Clearside Biomedical SCS Microinjector is currently only cleared by the FDA for its specific drug-device use to deliver triamcinolone acetonide for macular oedema associated with uveitis. It has not received FDA approval as a standalone device. In addition, the microneedle technology must demonstrate a high safety profile, account for variability in human eye tissue (predictable penetration depth and mechanical performance across diverse tissue types), and deliver precise, uniform doses. Sterilisation and manufacturing control must be established. Interpretation of animal data to predict human performance can be challenging, and robust early human feasibility studies may be necessary before conducting clinical trials.

caption.p: Clinical trials of microneedles to treat eye diseases.

sec.title: Current challenges and Future directions
sec.p: Microneedles have the potential to improve therapy by enhanced pharmacological delivery to the target site with minimal complications. Since the introduction of microneedles for dermatological applications in the 1970s, various types have been developed, ranging from solid to biodegradable microneedles. The ease of use of transdermal microneedle-based drug delivery systems has accelerated their advancement over the past two decades (Lee et al. 2020). As microneedle technology has progressed, microneedles have become more structurally flexible, enabling them to adapt to the complex surfaces of various tissues. Their ability to penetrate both soft and hard target tissues has significantly improved, and new functionalities have been developed for collecting diagnostic samples and for sensor applications. As a result of these advancements, microneedles are now widely used in various biomedical fields beyond just transdermal applications, including medication delivery, vaccine administration, disease diagnosis, and cosmeceuticals (Quinn et al. 2014; McCrudden et al. 2015; Xu et al. 2022).

Despite recent advances, several concerns need to be addressed to apply microneedle-based ocular treatments. It is important to investigate microneedle design specifications to reduce discomfort and complications, thereby improving patient acceptance and adherence as well as modulating drug release kinetics. Recent studies have explored the integration of advanced technologies, such as artificial intelligence and machine learning algorithms to optimise microneedle designs and drug release behaviour before the fabrication of microneedles (Biswas et al. 2024; He et al. 2024). This can help effectively optimise and increase the efficiency of microneedle development by reducing trial and error.

Proper biomaterial selection for microneedles that maintains the mechanical strength for insertion and ensures biocompatibility is another requirement. For example, the softening of biodegradable microneedles before insertion is one of the significant concerns, as the wet environment of the eye could reduce their mechanical strength (Le et al. 2023). In addition, repeated microneedle injections or the degradation of microneedles could lead to the accumulation of needle materials in ocular tissues potentially resulting in side effects like eye irritation (Lloyd et al. 2001). Limited long term safety data of ocular microneedles is another concern for microneedle-based ocular treatments. Most studies of microneedles have been conducted in vitro and in vivo only during short term period. Therefore, long-term, comprehensive clinical trials are required to confirm the safety of these treatments and support the introduction of microneedles into the pharmaceutical market, as current information is limited.

New designs of microneedle applicators should be developed to obtain reliable insertion for ocular drug delivery systems. Various microneedle applicator designs have been studied in dermatology to achieve consistent microneedle insertion (Leone et al. 2018; Kim et al. 2022). However, most studies have been used thumb pressing to insert microneedles into eye tissue. Only Ryu’s group has reported using a spring-loaded piston to insert single microneedle tip into the eye and no other applicator for microneedle use in ophthalmology has been reported yet (Song et al. 2015; Park et al. 2018). Therefore, new applicator designs should be capable of adjusting position, speed, and force as alternatives to the manual thumb press and spring-loaded piston.

In addition, the lack of an optimised manufacturing process and standardised regulations remains a barrier to the development of microneedle-based ocular treatments. Manufacturing microneedles is generally more complex than producing traditional dosage forms, such as eye drops or oral medications. For effective drug delivery while minimising tissue damage, it is crucial to control the height of microneedle tips to achieve appropriate penetration depth tailored to individual patients. In addition, advancements in materials science and mechanical engineering are needed to modulate drug loading efficiency and release kinetics, thereby maintaining the desired therapeutic concentration at the target site. The absence of consistent and comprehensive guidelines for ocular microneedles further hinders their clinical translation. To address these challenges, it is essential to establish standardised practices for both the manufacturing and clinical application of microneedles.

By introducing stimuli-responsive microneedles, the microneedles can enhance safety and therapeutic effectiveness by adjusting drug kinetics in response to specific triggers from the microenvironment of eye. The stimulus responsive release behaviours can be achieved by triggered external or internal stimuli such as pH, temperature and light. This method provides precise drug release control, which makes it suitable for eye treatments that require drug delivery on demand. A recent study reported the effectiveness of pH-responsive microneedles for the treatment of bacterial keratitis (Jiang et al. 2024). Ag was encapsulated within a zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (Ag@ZIF-8). Under acidic conditions (pH 5.6), mimicking the microenvironment of an infected cornea, Zn2+ and Ag+ ions were rapidly released within 12 hours. In contrast, almost no ion was released at physiological pH (7.4). ZIF-8 was embedded within the core of microneedles, and it was quickly released in PBS. These microneedles demonstrate enhanced antibacterial efficacy in a rat model of bacterial keratitis. Another recent study developed microneedles made from a thermally responsive hydrogel composed of poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) and graphene oxide (GO). The microneedles facilitate the thermally triggered release of riboflavin under the exposure of near-infra-red (NIR) laser at 808 nm (Hu et al. 2024). In vitro experiments showed that riboflavin was rapidly released within the first 30 minutes of NIR exposure cycles. After 2 hours, around 60% more riboflavin was released from the microneedles during NIR exposure cycles compared to conditions without irradiation. In addition, the microneedle drug delivery system showed promising therapeutic results in a rabbit keratoconus model.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Ophthalmic microneedles present a promising strategy to address the limitations of conventional ocular drug delivery methods, including the low bioavailability of eye drops and the invasiveness of intravitreal injections. Coated, hollow, dissolving, and swellable microneedles, fabricated from materials such as silicon, metals, and polymers, have demonstrated improved drug delivery capabilities across various preclinical models, including in vitro, ex vivo, and in vivo systems. In addition, the administration of triamcinolone acetonide injectable suspension (Clearside Biomedical, USA) using the SCS Microinjector has been approved by FDA, supporting the safety and feasibility of microneedle-based ocular treatments. Advancements in microneedle design, material selection, drug loading strategies, scalable manufacturing techniques, standardised regulations and long-term safety profile will support the development of innovative ocular drug delivery technologies, which could substantially improve current treatment modalities in the near future.
